Abelisauridae.description: Abelisauridae is a family of ceratosaurian theropod dinosaurs that were dominant predators in Gondwanan landmasses during the Cretaceous period. Abelisaurids are characterized by short, deep skulls, heavily ornamented cranial bones, and dramatically reduced forelimbs that are even more vestigial than those of tyrannosaurids. Notable members include Carnotaurus, Majungasaurus, and Abelisaurus. The family was most diverse in South America, Madagascar, and India.
diagnostic_features: Short, deep skull with rugose ornamentation on frontal and nasal bones
Extremely reduced forelimbs with vestigial digits
Thickened skull roof bones
Robust and short cervical vertebrae
Well-developed femoral musculature attachment sites indicating powerful hindlimbs

references.title: Abelisaurus comahuensis, n.g., n.sp., Carnosauria del Cretacico Tardio de Patagonia

Allosauridae.description: Allosauridae is a family of large carnosaur theropod dinosaurs best known from the Late Jurassic of North America and Europe. The family is typified by Allosaurus, one of the most abundant and well-studied large predatory dinosaurs. Allosaurids were apex predators characterized by large skulls with prominent lacrimal horns, powerful forelimbs with three clawed digits, and laterally compressed serrated teeth.
diagnostic_features: Prominent lacrimal horns or hornlets above the eyes
Large skull with paired nasal crests
Laterally compressed, recurved, serrated teeth
Three-fingered manus with large manual unguals
Robust cervical vertebrae with well-developed epipophyses

references.title: Notice of new dinosaurian reptiles

Ankylopollexia.description: Ankylopollexia, meaning "fused thumb," is a clade of ornithopod dinosaurs characterized by a robust, conical thumb spike formed by a modified and enlarged pollex (first manual digit). This distinctive feature likely served as a defensive weapon or for intraspecific combat. Ankylopollexians range from medium-sized bipedal forms such as Camptosaurus to the large iguanodonts and hadrosaurs. The clade represents one of the most diverse and successful ornithischian radiations.
diagnostic_features: Conical, spike-like thumb (pollex) formed by a fused and enlarged first manual ungual
Robust metacarpal I fused to or tightly articulating with the carpus
Progressive reduction of manual digit V
Elongate and robust prepubic process
Trend toward increasingly complex dental batteries in derived members

references.title: Phylogeny of the bird-hipped dinosaurs (Order Ornithischia)

Ankylosauria.description: Ankylosauria is a clade of heavily armored thyreophoran ornithischian dinosaurs known from the Middle Jurassic to the end of the Cretaceous. Ankylosaurs were low-slung, quadrupedal herbivores encased in extensive dermal armor consisting of osteoderms arranged in transverse rows across the body, often supplemented by lateral spines. Some ankylosaurs, particularly ankylosaurids, possessed a massive bony tail club used for defense. The clade includes Ankylosauridae and Nodosauridae.
diagnostic_features: Extensive dermal armor covering the dorsal surface and flanks
Low, wide body plan with short, robust limbs
Fused osteoderms on the skull forming a solid cranial shield
Tail club present in Ankylosauridae (modified distal caudal vertebrae and osteoderms)
Laterally projecting cervical or shoulder spines in many taxa
Secondary palate present

references.title: Two Lower Cretaceous dinosaurs of Mongolia

Avetheropoda.description: Avetheropoda is a clade of advanced theropod dinosaurs that unites Carnosauria and Coelurosauria. Avetheropods represent the more derived tetanurans, distinguished from megalosauroids by features of the skull, vertebral column, and limbs. The clade encompasses the majority of Late Jurassic and Cretaceous theropod diversity, including all living birds.
diagnostic_features: Maxillary fenestra present and well-developed
Reduced or absent prefrontal bone
Enlarged orbit relative to skull length
Fused interdental plates
Opisthopubic or mesopubic pelvis in derived members

references.title: Predatory Dinosaurs of the World

Brachiosauridae.description: Brachiosauridae is a family of macronarian sauropod dinosaurs known from the Late Jurassic to Early Cretaceous of North America, Africa, Europe, and possibly South America. Brachiosaurids are distinguished by their unusual body proportions, with forelimbs longer than or subequal to hindlimbs, giving them a distinctive giraffe-like elevated posture. This configuration allowed them to browse at greater heights than most other sauropods. Notable members include Brachiosaurus and Giraffatitan.
diagnostic_features: Forelimbs longer than or subequal in length to hindlimbs
Elevated, giraffe-like body posture with the shoulders higher than the hips
Elongated humerus exceeding femoral length
Highly elevated narial region on the skull
Spatulate teeth arranged along the full extent of the jaw margins
Relatively short tail compared to other sauropods

references.title: Brachiosaurus altithorax, the largest known dinosaur

Carcharodontosauridae.description: Carcharodontosauridae, meaning "shark-toothed lizard family," is a family of giant carnosaur theropod dinosaurs known from the Cretaceous of Africa, South America, Europe, Asia, and North America. The family includes some of the largest terrestrial predators ever to have lived, such as Carcharodontosaurus, Giganotosaurus, and Mapusaurus. They are named for their distinctive large, laterally compressed teeth with prominent serrations resembling those of Carcharodon (great white shark).
diagnostic_features: Large, laterally compressed teeth with pronounced, shark-like serrations
Very large body size, often exceeding 10 metres in length
Prominent ornamentation on lacrimal and postorbital bones
Pneumatized cranial bones with extensive sinus systems
Elongate and narrow maxilla

references.title: Wirbeltier-Reste der Baharije-Stufe (unterstes Cenoman). 10. Ein Skelett-Rest von Carcharodontosaurus nov. gen.
references.journal: Abhandlungen der Bayerischen Akademie der Wissenschaften, Mathematisch-naturwissenschaftliche Abteilung

Carnosauria.description: Carnosauria is a clade of large-bodied predatory theropod dinosaurs within Avetheropoda. In modern phylogenetic usage, Carnosauria is largely synonymous with Allosauroidea and includes the allosaurids, carcharodontosaurids, and their relatives. Carnosaurs were apex predators during the Jurassic and Early to mid-Cretaceous periods, with some reaching body sizes comparable to or exceeding Tyrannosaurus.
diagnostic_features: Large body size with proportionally large skull
Lacrimal bone with pneumatic recesses and ornamentation
Pronounced cnemial crest on the tibia
Large, laterally compressed, serrated teeth
Robust and well-developed forelimbs with three-fingered hands

references.title: Bemerkungen zur Systematik und Stammesgeschichte einiger Reptilien
references.journal: Zeitschrift fur Induktive Abstammungs- und Vererbungslehre

Cerapoda.description: Cerapoda is a clade of ornithischian dinosaurs that unites Ornithopoda and Marginocephalia. Cerapodans are characterized by specialized jaw mechanics adapted for efficient oral processing of plant material, including a well- developed coronoid process on the dentary, asymmetrical enamel on the teeth enabling precise occlusion, and the loss of premaxillary teeth. The clade encompasses a wide range of herbivorous dinosaurs from small bipeds to giant quadrupedal forms.
diagnostic_features: Well-developed coronoid process on the dentary
Asymmetrical enamel distribution on teeth facilitating shearing occlusion
Reduction or loss of premaxillary teeth
Diastema between premaxillary and maxillary tooth rows
Specialized jaw mechanics for efficient oral processing of vegetation

references.title: Phylogeny of the bird-hipped dinosaurs (Order Ornithischia)

Ceratopsia.description: Ceratopsia, meaning "horned faces," is a clade of marginocephalian ornithischian dinosaurs characterized by a unique rostral bone at the tip of the upper jaw and a parietosquamosal frill extending from the back of the skull. The clade includes small, bipedal basal forms such as Psittacosaurus and large, quadrupedal ceratopsids like Triceratops and Styracosaurus. Ceratopsians were exclusively herbivorous and were most diverse in the Late Cretaceous of North America and Asia. Horns above the eyes and nose evolved in more derived members.
diagnostic_features: Rostral bone forming a sharp, beak-like tip of the upper jaw
Parietosquamosal frill extending posterodorsally from the skull
Jugal flanges (flared cheek horns) on the skull
Shearing dentition with double-rooted cheek teeth
Nasal and supraorbital horns in derived ceratopsids
Predentary bone with a sharp cutting edge

references.title: Additional characters of the Ceratopsidae, with notice of new Cretaceous dinosaurs

Ceratopsidae.description: Ceratopsidae is a family of large-bodied ceratopsian ornithischian dinosaurs known from the Late Cretaceous of western North America (and possibly Asia). Ceratopsids are among the most recognizable dinosaurs, characterized by elaborate cranial ornamentation including large nasal and supraorbital horns, expansive parietosquamosal frills often decorated with epiossifications (epoccipitals and epiparietals), and a robust, barrel-shaped body. The family is divided into Chasmosaurinae (long-frilled forms like Triceratops) and Centrosaurinae (short-frilled forms like Centrosaurus).
diagnostic_features: Large parietosquamosal frill with marginal epiossifications (epoccipitals)
Prominent nasal horn and/or paired supraorbital horns
Very large skull, often exceeding 1.5 metres in length
Double-rooted, shearing cheek teeth arranged in dental batteries
Obligate quadrupedal stance with robust, graviportal limbs
Syncervical (fused anterior cervical vertebrae) supporting the massive skull

references.title: Additional characters of the Ceratopsidae, with notice of new Cretaceous dinosaurs

Ceratosauria.description: Ceratosauria is a clade of early-diverging theropod dinosaurs that radiated primarily during the Jurassic and Cretaceous periods. Many ceratosaurs possessed prominent cranial ornamentation, including horns and crests. The group was particularly diverse in Gondwanan landmasses and includes the abelisaurids, noasaurids, and the Jurassic ceratosaurids. Ceratosaurs generally retained four digits on the hand, unlike the three-fingered condition of more derived theropods.
diagnostic_features: Cranial ornamentation including horns, bosses, or rugose crests in many taxa
Fused pelvic elements (ilium, ischium, pubis) in mature individuals
Four functional digits on the manus in basal members
Specialized sacral vertebral articulations
Distinctive cervical vertebral morphology with low neural spines

references.title: Principal characters of American Jurassic dinosaurs, Part VIII

Coelurosauria.description: Coelurosauria is a large and diverse clade of theropod dinosaurs that includes tyrannosaurs, ornithomimosaurs, maniraptorans, and all living birds. Coelurosaurs are the most speciose theropod clade and exhibit an extraordinary range of body sizes, diets, and ecologies. Many coelurosaurs possessed filamentous integumentary structures or true feathers. The clade originated in the Middle Jurassic and includes the only dinosaur lineage to survive the end-Cretaceous extinction.
diagnostic_features: Elongate distal caudal vertebrae with reduced or absent transverse processes
Filamentous integument or feathers present in many lineages
Reduced or absent prefrontal bone
Semilunate carpal bone in wrist of many derived taxa
Tibiotarsus longer than femur
Enlarged cerebrum relative to body size

references.title: Das naturliche System der Saurischia
references.journal: Centralblatt fur Mineralogie, Geologie und Palaontologie B

Dinosauria.description: Dinosauria is a clade of archosaurian reptiles that first appeared during the Triassic period, approximately 233 million years ago. Dinosaurs are united by a suite of anatomical features including a fully upright (parasagittal) stance, a fully open (perforated) acetabulum in the pelvis, and three or more sacral vertebrae. The clade encompasses two major lineages, Saurischia and Ornithischia, and includes all descendants of their most recent common ancestor, extending to modern birds (Aves).
diagnostic_features: Fully erect, parasagittal hindlimb posture
Perforated (fully open) acetabulum
Three or more sacral vertebrae
Elongate deltopectoral crest on the humerus
Asymmetrical fourth trochanter on the femur
Open hip socket allowing pillar-erect stance

references.title: Report on British fossil reptiles, Part II
references.journal: Report of the British Association for the Advancement of Science

Diplodocidae.description: Diplodocidae is a family of diplodocoid sauropod dinosaurs known primarily from the Late Jurassic of North America, with additional occurrences in Africa and Europe. Diplodocids are characterized by their extremely elongated bodies with long, whip-like tails, elongate and shallow skulls with anteriorly restricted pencil-shaped teeth, and bifurcated (forked) neural spines on cervical and dorsal vertebrae. Notable members include Diplodocus, Apatosaurus, and Brontosaurus.
diagnostic_features: Extremely elongated tail with whip-like distal section
Pencil-shaped (cylindrical) teeth restricted to the front of the jaws
Bifurcated (forked) neural spines on cervical and anterior dorsal vertebrae
Anteroposteriorly elongated skull with squared-off snout
Elongated cervical vertebrae with low neural arches
Tail chevrons with anterior and posterior projections (double-beamed)

references.title: Principal characters of American Jurassic dinosaurs, Part VIII

Dromaeosauridae.description: Dromaeosauridae is a family of small to medium-sized maniraptoran theropod dinosaurs commonly known as "raptors." Dromaeosaurids were agile, feathered predators characterized by their signature enlarged, sickle-shaped retractable claw on the second toe (pedal digit II), stiffened tails used for balance during rapid pursuit, and well-developed pennaceous feathers. The family includes well-known genera such as Velociraptor, Deinonychus, and Microraptor. Dromaeosaurids are among the closest non-avian relatives of birds.
diagnostic_features: Enlarged, hyperextensible, sickle-shaped ungual on pedal digit II
Stiffened tail with elongated prezygapophyses and chevrons
Pennaceous feathers on forelimbs and hindlimbs
Semilunate carpal bone enabling wing-folding motion of manus
Laterally compressed, recurved, serrated teeth
Reduced and stiffened distal tail functioning as a dynamic stabilizer

references.title: The family Deinodontidae, with notice of a new genus from the Cretaceous of Alberta
references.journal: Bulletin of the American Museum of Natural History

Hadrosauridae.description: Hadrosauridae is a family of large ornithopod dinosaurs commonly known as duck-billed dinosaurs. Hadrosaurs were the most diverse and abundant large herbivorous dinosaurs of the Late Cretaceous, with a global distribution. They are characterized by highly specialized dental batteries consisting of hundreds of closely packed teeth forming a continuous grinding surface, broad and flat edentulous premaxillary bills, and in the subfamily Lambeosaurinae, elaborate hollow cranial crests likely used for vocalization and display. Hadrosaurs include the two major subgroups Hadrosaurinae (flat-headed forms) and Lambeosaurinae (crested forms).
diagnostic_features: Highly specialized dental batteries with hundreds of interlocking teeth
Broad, spatulate, edentulous premaxillary bill
Hollow cranial crests in Lambeosaurinae (extensions of nasal passages)
Complete loss of the thumb spike
Extensive ossified tendons forming a lattice along the vertebral column
Hoof-like unguals on weight-bearing digits

references.title: Synopsis of the extinct Batrachia, Reptilia, and Aves of North America
references.journal: Transactions of the American Philosophical Society

Hadrosauriformes.description: Hadrosauriformes is a clade of styracosternan ornithopod dinosaurs that includes the hadrosaurs (duck-billed dinosaurs) and their immediate relatives such as Iguanodon. Hadrosauriforms are characterized by well- developed dental batteries consisting of multiple stacked replacement teeth, broad and flattened snouts (often described as duck-billed), and large body size. The clade was globally distributed during the Cretaceous period and represents one of the most successful ornithischian radiations.
diagnostic_features: Well-developed dental battery with multiple replacement teeth in each tooth position
Broad, flattened premaxilla forming a duck-like bill
Reduced or absent thumb spike compared to basal styracosternans
Elongate and dorsoventrally flattened rostrum
Three or more replacement teeth per tooth family

references.title: The origin and evolution of dinosaurs
references.journal: Annual Review of Earth and Planetary Sciences

Macronaria.description: Macronaria, meaning "large nostrils," is a clade of neosauropod dinosaurs characterized by large nasal openings (external nares) that are typically larger than the orbits. The clade includes brachiosaurids, titanosaurs, and their relatives, and was particularly diverse and dominant during the Cretaceous period. Macronarians tended to have more robust, box-like skulls with spatulate teeth, in contrast to the elongate skulls and peg-like teeth of diplodocoids.
diagnostic_features: Enlarged external nares larger than the orbit
Spatulate (spoon-shaped) teeth
Box-like, dorsoventrally deep skull
Opisthocoelous anterior caudal vertebrae
Metacarpals arranged in a tubular, U-shaped colonnade

references.title: Early evolution and higher-level phylogeny of sauropod dinosaurs

Maniraptora.description: Maniraptora, meaning "hand snatchers," is a clade of coelurosaurian theropod dinosaurs that includes dromaeosaurids, troodontids, oviraptorosaurs, therizinosaurs, and birds (Aves). Maniraptorans are characterized by elongated arms relative to body size, a semilunate carpal bone enabling a folding mechanism in the wrist, and the widespread presence of pennaceous feathers. The clade represents the closest relatives of and includes modern birds, making it one of the most significant groups in understanding avian origins.
diagnostic_features: Elongated forelimbs relative to body size
Semilunate (half-moon shaped) carpal bone allowing wrist folding
Pennaceous feathers present in many lineages
Elongate and mobile manus
Opisthopubic pelvis (posteriorly directed pubis) in many taxa
Reduced or absent postorbital bar

references.title: Saurischian monophyly and the origin of birds
references.journal: Memoirs of the California Academy of Sciences

Marginocephalia.description: Marginocephalia, meaning "fringed heads," is a clade of cerapodan ornithischian dinosaurs united by the presence of a distinctive bony shelf or frill projecting from the posterior margin of the skull. The clade includes Pachycephalosauria (dome-headed dinosaurs) and Ceratopsia (horned dinosaurs). Marginocephalians were exclusively herbivorous and ranged from small bipedal forms to massive quadrupedal ceratopsids. The group was most diverse in the Late Cretaceous of North America and Asia.
diagnostic_features: Parietosquamosal shelf or frill projecting from the posterior margin of the skull
Rostral bone at the tip of the upper jaw (in ceratopsians) or thickened skull roof (in pachycephalosaurs)
Short postorbital region of the skull
Reduction of the supratemporal fenestra
Herbivorous dentition with leaf-shaped or shearing teeth

references.title: Phylogeny of the bird-hipped dinosaurs (Order Ornithischia)

Megalosauridae.description: Megalosauridae is a family of large basal tetanuran theropod dinosaurs known primarily from the Middle to Late Jurassic of Europe, Africa, and South America. The family includes Megalosaurus bucklandii, the first dinosaur to be scientifically described and named (1824). Megalosaurids were robust, large- bodied predators with powerful forelimbs and laterally compressed, serrated teeth.
diagnostic_features: Large body size with robust skeletal construction
Laterally compressed, serrated teeth
Well-developed and powerful forelimbs with large manual claws
Tall neural spines on dorsal vertebrae
Distinctive premaxillary-maxillary contact morphology

references.title: On the upper jaw of Megalosaurus
references.journal: Quarterly Journal of the Geological Society of London

Megalosauroidea.description: Megalosauroidea is a superfamily of large-bodied basal tetanuran theropods that includes Megalosauridae and Spinosauridae. These predators were prominent during the Middle Jurassic through Late Cretaceous. Megalosauroids were among the first dinosaurs to be scientifically described and include some of the most specialized predatory forms, such as the semi-aquatic spinosaurids.
diagnostic_features: Large body size in most members
Elongate premaxilla-maxilla contact
Robust skull construction with well-developed antorbital fenestra
Elongate and robust manual unguals
Basal tetanuran grade of skeletal organization

references.authors: Holtz, T.R., Molnar, R.E. & Currie, P.J.
references.title: Basal Tetanurae

Noasauridae.description: Noasauridae is a family of small to medium-sized ceratosaurian theropod dinosaurs known primarily from Gondwanan continents during the Cretaceous period. Noasaurids were lightly built, gracile predators or omnivores, and represent the smaller-bodied counterpart to their sister group Abelisauridae within Abelisauria. Notable members include Noasaurus, Masiakasaurus, and Elaphrosaurus.
diagnostic_features: Small to medium body size with gracile build
Lightly constructed skull
Elongate cervical vertebrae relative to abelisaurids
Procumbent (forward-projecting) teeth in some taxa
Relatively long and slender hindlimbs

references.title: A continental assemblage of tetrapods from the Upper Cretaceous beds of El Brete, northwestern Argentina (Sauropoda-Coelurosauria-Carnosauria-Aves)
references.journal: Memoires de la Societe Geologique de France

Ornithischia.description: Ornithischia, meaning "bird-hipped," is one of the two traditionally recognized major clades of dinosaurs. Ornithischians are united by a distinctive pelvic anatomy in which the pubis is rotated posteriorly to lie parallel with the ischium, convergently resembling the pelvic arrangement of birds (though birds are actually saurischians). The clade includes the armored thyreophorans (stegosaurs and ankylosaurs), the horned ceratopsians, the dome-headed pachycephalosaurs, and the duck-billed ornithopods. All ornithischians were herbivorous.
diagnostic_features: Opisthopubic pelvis with posteriorly directed pubis parallel to ischium
Predentary bone at the tip of the lower jaw
Ossified tendons along the vertebral column in many taxa
Leaf-shaped cheek teeth with denticulate margins
Palpebral bone in the orbit
Herbivorous diet across all members

references.title: On the classification of the fossil animals commonly named Dinosauria
references.journal: Proceedings of the Royal Society of London

Ornithomimosauria.description: Ornithomimosauria, meaning "bird-mimic lizards," is a clade of coelurosaurian theropod dinosaurs known from the Cretaceous of North America, Asia, Europe, and Africa. Often called "ostrich dinosaurs," ornithomimosaurs were characterized by long legs adapted for swift running, elongate necks, small skulls with toothless beaks (in derived forms), and large eyes. They were likely omnivorous or herbivorous, representing a significant ecological departure from the ancestral carnivorous theropod condition.
diagnostic_features: Toothless, keratinous beak in derived members
Elongate cervical vertebrae forming a long, S-curved neck
Large orbits with well-developed sclerotic rings
Long, gracile hindlimbs adapted for cursorial locomotion
Arctometatarsalian foot in derived forms
Elongate manus with subequal metacarpals

references.title: Ostrich dinosaurs from the Late Cretaceous of western Canada

Ornithopoda.description: Ornithopoda, meaning "bird feet," is a clade of cerapodan ornithischian dinosaurs that includes the iguanodonts and hadrosaurs along with numerous smaller, basal members. Ornithopods were among the most successful and widespread herbivorous dinosaurs, ranging from small bipedal forms in the Jurassic to large quadrupedal hadrosaurs in the Late Cretaceous. They are characterized by their efficient jaw mechanism that allowed transverse (pleurokinetic) chewing of plant material, a feature rare among reptiles.
diagnostic_features: Pleurokinetic skull allowing lateral movement of the maxillae during chewing
Tridactyl (three-toed) pes with hoof-like unguals
Absence of premaxillary teeth (keratinous beak present)
Well-developed dental battery in advanced forms
Tendency toward facultative or obligate quadrupedality in larger taxa
Elongate prepubic process of the pubis

references.title: Principal characters of American Jurassic dinosaurs, Part IV

Pachycephalosauria.description: Pachycephalosauria, meaning "thick-headed lizards," is a clade of marginocephalian ornithischian dinosaurs characterized by a dramatically thickened skull roof (frontoparietal dome). These bipedal herbivores or omnivores were most diverse during the Late Cretaceous of North America and Asia. The thickened dome has been hypothesized to have served in intraspecific head-butting or flank-butting contests, though this remains debated. Notable genera include Pachycephalosaurus, Stegoceras, and Dracorex.
diagnostic_features: Dramatically thickened frontoparietal dome on the skull roof
Peripheral ornamentation of nodes and horns around the dome margin
Wide, shelf-like squamosal
Relatively large orbits
Bipedal stance with stiffened tail
Broad body with flared iliac blades

references.title: Pachycephalosauria, a new suborder of ornithischian dinosaurs

Saurischia.description: Saurischia, meaning "lizard-hipped," is one of the two major clades of dinosaurs. Saurischians retain an ancestral pelvic configuration in which the pubis points forward and downward, in contrast to the retroverted pubis of ornithischians. The clade includes the bipedal, mostly carnivorous theropods and the long-necked, herbivorous sauropodomorphs. Saurischia is defined as all dinosaurs more closely related to birds than to Triceratops.
diagnostic_features: Forward-pointing (anteroventrally directed) pubis in basal members
Elongate, mobile neck with cervical vertebrae bearing well-developed pleurocoels
Asymmetrical hand with digit II longest
Subnarial foramen present between premaxilla and maxilla

references.title: On the classification of the fossil animals commonly named Dinosauria
references.journal: Proceedings of the Royal Society of London

Sauropoda.description: Sauropoda is a clade of large to gigantic sauropodomorph dinosaurs that includes the largest terrestrial animals in Earth's history. Sauropods were obligate quadrupeds with columnar limbs, extremely elongated necks containing 12 or more cervical vertebrae, long tails, and relatively small skulls. Extensive skeletal pneumaticity (air-filled bones) reduced body mass and may have aided respiration. Sauropods were globally distributed throughout the Jurassic and Cretaceous periods and occupied large-bodied herbivore niches on every continent.
diagnostic_features: Gigantic body size with columnar, graviportal limbs
Extremely elongated neck with 12 or more opisthocoelous cervical vertebrae
Extensive postcranial skeletal pneumaticity
Spatulate or peg-like teeth
Obligate quadrupedal posture
Reduced or absent manual phalanges on lateral digits

references.title: Principal characters of American Jurassic dinosaurs, Part I

Sauropodomorpha.description: Sauropodomorpha is a major clade of saurischian dinosaurs comprising the long-necked herbivorous dinosaurs and their ancestors. The clade ranges from small, facultatively bipedal early forms of the Late Triassic to the giant quadrupedal sauropods that dominated terrestrial herbivore niches throughout the Jurassic and Cretaceous. Sauropodomorphs include the largest land animals ever to have lived and are characterized by their elongated necks, small heads relative to body size, and herbivorous or omnivorous diets.
diagnostic_features: Elongated neck with ten or more cervical vertebrae
Small skull relative to body size
Leaf-shaped or spatulate teeth adapted for herbivory
Enlarged nares
Large pollex (thumb) claw on manus digit I
Trend toward increasing body size through evolutionary history

references.title: Die fossile Reptil-Ordnung Saurischia, ihre Entwicklung und Geschichte

Spinosauridae.description: Spinosauridae is a family of large megalosauroid theropod dinosaurs characterized by elongated, crocodile-like snouts and conical, unserrated teeth adapted for piscivory (fish-eating). Many spinosaurids possessed enlarged dorsal neural spines forming a sail or ridge along the back. The family includes Spinosaurus, one of the largest known theropods, which shows evidence of semi-aquatic habits. Spinosaurids are known from the Cretaceous of Africa, Europe, and South America.
diagnostic_features: Elongated, narrow snout with a terminal rosette of premaxillary teeth
Conical, unserrated teeth adapted for grasping fish
Enlarged, elongate neural spines on dorsal and sacral vertebrae
Secondary palate partially developed
Enlarged manual ungual on digit I
Retracted external nares

references.title: Ergebnisse der Forschungsreisen Prof. E. Stromers in den Wusten Agyptens. II. Wirbeltier-Reste der Baharije-Stufe (unterstes Cenoman). 3. Das Original des Theropoden Spinosaurus aegyptiacus nov. gen., nov. spec.
references.journal: Abhandlungen der Koniglichen Bayerischen Akademie der Wissenschaften

Stegosauria.description: Stegosauria is a clade of thyreophoran ornithischian dinosaurs best known for their distinctive rows of dorsal plates and spines. Stegosaurs appeared in the Middle Jurassic and were most diverse during the Late Jurassic, with the group declining through the Cretaceous. They were medium to large quadrupedal herbivores with small heads, short necks, and hindlimbs significantly longer than forelimbs, giving the body a distinctive arched profile. The tail typically bore two pairs of elongate spines (the "thagomizer").
diagnostic_features: Parasagittal rows of alternating or paired dorsal plates and/or spines
Tail spines (thagomizer) used for defense
Hindlimbs substantially longer than forelimbs
Small skull with simple, leaf-shaped teeth and weak jaw musculature
Low, arched body profile
Relatively small brain (low encephalization quotient)

references.title: A new order of extinct Reptilia (Stegosauria) from the Jurassic of the Rocky Mountains

Styracosterna.description: Styracosterna, meaning "spiked stern," is a clade of ankylopollexian ornithopod dinosaurs characterized by a spike-like first manual ungual (thumb spike) that is more developed and conical than in basal ankylopollexians. The clade includes Iguanodon and more derived ornithopods leading to Hadrosauriformes. Styracosternans became the dominant large-bodied herbivores in many Early to Late Cretaceous ecosystems worldwide.
diagnostic_features: Well-developed, spike-like conical pollex ungual
Loss of manual digit V phalanges in many members
Elongate snout with expanded and edentulous premaxilla
Hoof-like unguals on pedal digits
Maxillary tooth rows set medially with a fleshy cheek inferred

references.title: Phylogeny of the bird-hipped dinosaurs (Order Ornithischia)

Tetanurae.description: Tetanurae, meaning "stiff tails," is a large clade of theropod dinosaurs characterized by stiffened tails with interlocking zygapophyses in the distal caudal vertebrae, and the reduction of the hand to three functional digits. Tetanurans include the megalosauroids, allosaurs, and coelurosaurs (including birds). The clade represents the majority of theropod diversity from the Middle Jurassic onward.
diagnostic_features: Stiffened distal tail with interlocking zygapophyses
Reduction of the hand to three functional digits (loss of digit IV)
Maxillary tooth row restricted to anterior portion of skull, not extending posterior to antorbital fenestra
Large, well-developed maxillary fenestra
Tibia longer than or equal to femur

references.title: Saurischian monophyly and the origin of birds
references.journal: Memoirs of the California Academy of Sciences

Therizinosauridae.description: Therizinosauridae is a family of bizarre, large-bodied maniraptoran theropod dinosaurs known from the Cretaceous of Asia and North America. Despite their theropod ancestry, therizinosaurs were herbivorous, possessing a suite of unusual adaptations including enormously elongated manual claws (up to 1 metre in Therizinosaurus), a wide, barrel-shaped torso for housing an enlarged herbivorous gut, a small head with a keratinous beak and leaf-shaped teeth, and a broad, four-toed foot.
diagnostic_features: Enormously elongated manual unguals (claws), especially on digit I
Wide, barrel-shaped torso accommodating an enlarged gut
Small skull with edentulous premaxilla and leaf-shaped cheek teeth
Opisthopubic (posteriorly directed) pelvis
Broad, functionally tetradactyl (four-toed) pes
Dense, filamentous integumentary covering

references.title: New turtle-like reptile in Mongolia

Theropoda.description: Theropoda, meaning "beast feet," is a clade of predominantly bipedal saurischian dinosaurs. Most theropods were carnivorous, though several lineages independently evolved herbivory or omnivory. Theropods are characterized by hollow (pneumatic) bones, three-toed weight-bearing hindlimbs, and a furcula (wishbone). The clade includes all living birds (Aves), making Theropoda one of the most successful vertebrate lineages in Earth's history.
diagnostic_features: Hollow (pneumatized) long bones
Obligate bipedal stance
Three weight-bearing digits on the pes (functionally tridactyl foot)
Furcula (fused clavicles) present
Intramandibular joint in the lower jaw
Carnivorous dentition with serrated, laterally compressed teeth in basal members

references.title: Principal characters of American Jurassic dinosaurs, Part V

Thyreophora.description: Thyreophora, meaning "shield bearers," is a clade of armored ornithischian dinosaurs that includes Stegosauria and Ankylosauria. Thyreophorans are characterized by the presence of dermal armor in the form of osteoderms (bony scutes, plates, or spines) embedded in the skin along the body. The clade appeared in the Early Jurassic and persisted until the end of the Cretaceous, with a global distribution. Thyreophorans were obligate quadrupeds and herbivores.
diagnostic_features: Parasagittal rows of osteoderms (dermal armor) along the dorsal surface
Keeled scutes or plates on the body
Quadrupedal stance in all but the most basal forms
Robust, low-slung body plan
Small head relative to body size with simple, leaf-shaped teeth

references.title: Die Dinosaurier der siebenburgischen Landesteile Ungarns
references.journal: Mitteilungen aus dem Jahrbuche der ungarischen geologischen Reichsanstalt

Titanosauria.description: Titanosauria is a diverse and species-rich clade of macronarian sauropod dinosaurs that dominated herbivore niches across Gondwana and Laurasia during the Cretaceous period. Titanosaurs include both the largest known dinosaurs (Argentinosaurus, Patagotitan) and some of the smallest sauropods (Magyarosaurus). Many titanosaurs possessed osteoderms (bony armor plates) embedded in the skin, a feature unique among sauropods. The clade was the last major sauropod radiation before the end-Cretaceous extinction.
diagnostic_features: Procoelous caudal vertebrae (concave anteriorly, convex posteriorly)
Osteoderms (bony dermal armor) present in many taxa
Wide-gauge (laterally splayed) limb posture
Loss of manual phalanges in derived forms
Strongly opisthocoelous dorsal and caudal vertebrae
Expanded iliac blade with a preacetabular process directed laterally

references.title: Notices of new and other Vertebrata from Indian Tertiary and Secondary rocks
references.journal: Records of the Geological Survey of India

Troodontidae.description: Troodontidae is a family of small, bird-like maniraptoran theropod dinosaurs known from the Cretaceous of North America, Asia, and Europe. Troodontids are notable for possessing the largest brain-to-body-size ratio among non-avian dinosaurs, suggesting relatively high intelligence. They had large, forward- facing eyes indicating good binocular vision, long and slender legs adapted for speed, and a retractable sickle claw on the second toe (smaller than in dromaeosaurids). Troodontids are among the closest known relatives of Aves.
diagnostic_features: Large brain relative to body size (high encephalization quotient)
Large, forward-facing orbits with well-developed binocular vision
Closely packed, numerous small teeth with large denticles
Retractable sickle claw on pedal digit II (smaller than dromaeosaurid condition)
Slender and elongate metatarsus adapted for cursorial locomotion
Asymmetric trochlea of metatarsal IV

references.title: A new coelurid dinosaur from the Belly River Cretaceous of Alberta
references.journal: Bulletin of the Canadian Department of Mines, Geological Survey

Tyrannosauridae.description: Tyrannosauridae is a family of large coelurosaurian theropod dinosaurs that were apex predators during the Late Cretaceous of North America and Asia. The family includes Tyrannosaurus rex, one of the largest and most powerful terrestrial predators known. Tyrannosaurids are characterized by their proportionally enormous skulls, massive bite force, robust heterodont dentition, extremely reduced two-fingered forelimbs, and powerful hindlimbs adapted for cursorial locomotion.
diagnostic_features: Proportionally enormous skull with robust, deeply rooted teeth
D-shaped cross-section of premaxillary teeth (incisiform premaxillary teeth)
Extremely reduced forelimbs with only two functional digits
Massive adductor musculature indicating powerful bite force
Arctometatarsalian foot with pinched third metatarsal
Fused nasals along the midline

references.title: Tyrannosaurus, Upper Cretaceous carnivorous dinosaur (second communication)
references.journal: Bulletin of the American Museum of Natural History

Ankylosaurus.description: A genus of ankylosaurid dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous of western North America. Ankylosaurus was the largest known ankylosaurid, covered in massive bony armor plates (osteoderms) and possessing a large club at the end of its tail. It was among the last non-avian dinosaurs, living alongside Tyrannosaurus and Triceratops.

features: Body covered in large, bony osteoderms
Massive tail club formed from fused osteoderms
Low, wide skull with triangular horns

diagnostic_features: Large tail club formed by fused caudal osteoderms
Triangular horns projecting from rear of skull
Extensive dorsal armor of keeled osteoderms

species.name: Ankylosaurus magniventris

holotype.material: top of skull, vertebrae, ribs, shoulder girdle, armor

species.description: The only species of Ankylosaurus. Known from several partial specimens from the latest Cretaceous formations of western North America.

references.title: The Ankylosauridae, a new family of armored dinosaurs from the Upper Cretaceous
references.journal: Bulletin of the American Museum of Natural History

references.title: Systematics, phylogeny and palaeobiogeography of the ankylosaurid dinosaurs

Brachiosaurus.description: A genus of sauropod dinosaur from the Late Jurassic of North America. Brachiosaurus is unusual among sauropods for its proportionally long forelimbs, giving it a giraffe-like posture with the body sloping upward toward the head. It was one of the tallest and largest dinosaurs known from relatively complete remains.

features: Long forelimbs giving a distinctive upward-sloping body profile
Tall, arched nasal crest on the skull
Extremely long neck held at a steep angle

diagnostic_features: Forelimbs longer than hindlimbs
Elongate metacarpals
Arched nasal bones forming a crest above the skull

species.name: Brachiosaurus altithorax
species.description: The only valid species of Brachiosaurus. Known from a partial skeleton discovered in the Morrison Formation of Colorado.

references.title: Brachiosaurus altithorax, the largest known dinosaur
A re-evaluation of Brachiosaurus altithorax Riggs 1903 (Dinosauria, Sauropoda) and its generic separation from Giraffatitan brancai (Janensch 1914)

Diplodocus.description: A genus of diplodocid sauropod dinosaur from the Late Jurassic of western North America. Diplodocus is among the most easily identifiable dinosaurs, with its classic sauropod shape of a long neck and tail, with four sturdy legs. It was one of the longer dinosaurs, with some species reaching over 25 metres in length.

features: Extremely long, whip-like tail
Long neck with a relatively small, elongated skull
Peg-like teeth restricted to the front of the jaws

diagnostic_features: Peg-like teeth restricted to anterior portion of jaws
Extremely elongated caudal vertebrae with double-beam chevrons
Anteriorly positioned nares

species.name: Diplodocus longus
species.description: The type species of Diplodocus, originally described from caudal vertebrae. The distinctive double-beam chevron bones give the genus its name.

species.name: Diplodocus carnegii
species.description: Known from one of the most complete sauropod skeletons ever found. Casts of the skeleton were distributed to museums worldwide, making it one of the most mounted dinosaur skeletons in the world.

references.title: Principal characters of American Jurassic dinosaurs, Part I
Diplodocus (Marsh): Its osteology, taxonomy, and probable habits, with a restoration of the skeleton

Iguanodon.description: A genus of ornithopod dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous of Europe. Iguanodon was one of the first dinosaurs ever scientifically described and is one of the founding members of the group Dinosauria. It is notable for its large thumb spike, which was originally mistakenly placed on its nose in early reconstructions.

features: Large conical thumb spike
Robust build with ability to walk bipedally or quadrupedally
Broad, blunt snout with a horny beak

diagnostic_features: Large conical thumb spike (manual digit I ungual)
Laterally compressed, leaf-shaped teeth with denticulate margins
Robust fifth finger that could oppose the others

species.name: Iguanodon bernissartensis

holotype.institution: Royal Belgian Institute of Natural Sciences

species.description: Known from over 30 complete skeletons discovered in a coal mine at Bernissart, Belgium. One of the best-known ornithopod dinosaurs.

references.title: Notice on the Iguanodon, a newly discovered fossil reptile
references.journal: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society of London

references.title: Basal Iguanodontia

Parasaurolophus.description: A genus of hadrosaurid dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous of North America. Parasaurolophus is best known for its large, elaborate cranial crest that extends back from the skull in a long, curved tube. The hollow crest likely functioned as a resonating chamber for producing sounds and for visual display.

features: Long, curved tubular cranial crest extending behind the skull
Broad, duck-like bill
Deep tail for possible display

diagnostic_features: Long, posteriorly directed tubular cranial crest
Crest contains convoluted nasal passages
Elongate, backward-curving premaxillary-nasal crest

species.name: Parasaurolophus walkeri

holotype.material: partial skeleton with nearly complete skull

species.description: The type species of Parasaurolophus, known from a partial skeleton with a nearly complete skull preserving the distinctive tubular crest.

references.title: Parasaurolophus walkeri, a new genus and species of crested trachodont dinosaur
references.journal: University of Toronto Studies, Geological Series

references.authors: Evans, D.C.; Bavington, R.; Campione, N.E.
references.title: An unusual hadrosaurid braincase from the Dinosaur Park Formation and the biostratigraphy of Parasaurolophus (Ornithischia: Lambeosaurinae) from southern Alberta

Spinosaurus.description: A genus of spinosaurid dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous of North Africa. Spinosaurus may have been the largest of all known carnivorous dinosaurs, even larger than Tyrannosaurus. It is distinguished by the tall neural spines on its vertebrae, which formed a prominent sail or hump along its back. Recent discoveries suggest it was semi-aquatic, adapted for hunting fish.

features: Tall neural spine sail along the back
Elongated, crocodile-like snout
Conical teeth adapted for catching fish
Paddle-like tail for swimming

diagnostic_features: Extremely elongated neural spines forming a dorsal sail
Elongated rostrum with conical, unserrated teeth
Retracted nares positioned far back on the skull

species.name: Spinosaurus aegyptiacus

holotype.institution: Bayerische Staatssammlung fur Palaontologie und Geologie

species.description: The type species of Spinosaurus. The original holotype was destroyed during a British bombing raid on Munich in 1944. Recent discoveries in Morocco have provided new material revealing its semi-aquatic adaptations.

references.title: Ergebnisse der Forschungsreisen Prof. E. Stromers in den Wusten Agyptens. II. Wirbeltier-Reste der Baharije-Stufe
references.journal: Abhandlungen der Koniglichen Bayerischen Akademie der Wissenschaften

references.authors: Ibrahim, N.; Sereno, P.C.; Dal Sasso, C.; Maganuco, S.; Fabbri, M.; Martill, D.M.; Zouhri, S.; Myhrvold, N.; Iurino, D.A.
references.title: Semiaquatic adaptations in a giant predatory dinosaur

Stegosaurus.description: A genus of armored dinosaur from the Late Jurassic of North America and Europe. Stegosaurus is immediately recognizable by the distinctive double row of kite-shaped plates along its back and the two pairs of long spikes on its tail, known as the thagomizer.

features: Double row of large, kite-shaped dorsal plates
Two pairs of tail spikes (thagomizer)
Small, narrow head relative to body size

diagnostic_features: Alternating dorsal plates arranged in two rows
Four tail spikes (thagomizer)
Extremely small braincase relative to body size

species.name: Stegosaurus stenops

holotype.institution: Smithsonian National Museum of Natural History

species.description: The best-known species of Stegosaurus, known from several nearly complete skeletons from the Morrison Formation.

references.title: New order of extinct Reptilia (Stegosauria) from the Jurassic of the Rocky Mountains

references.authors: Maidment, S.C.R.; Norman, D.B.; Barrett, P.M.; Upchurch, P.
references.title: Systematics and phylogeny of Stegosauria (Dinosauria: Ornithischia)

Triceratops.description: A genus of chasmosaurine ceratopsian dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous of North America. One of the last and largest ceratopsians, Triceratops is distinguished by its large bony frill and three facial horns. It is one of the most recognizable of all dinosaurs and among the most commonly found in the fossil record.

features: Large bony frill extending from the back of the skull
Two long brow horns above the eyes
Short nasal horn on the snout

diagnostic_features: Three facial horns with elongate brow horns
Solid parietal frill lacking fenestrae
Elongate squamosal bones forming frill margin

species.name: Triceratops horridus
species.description: The type species of Triceratops, originally described from a skull roof with horn cores found in Colorado.

species.name: Triceratops prorsus
species.description: A second valid species of Triceratops, distinguished from T. horridus by differences in horn and frill morphology.

references.title: Notice of gigantic horned Dinosauria from the Cretaceous

references.authors: Scannella, J.B.; Fowler, D.W.; Goodwin, M.B.; Horner, J.R.
references.title: Evolutionary trends in Triceratops from the Hell Creek Formation, Montana
references.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

Tyrannosaurus.description: A genus of large theropod dinosaur that lived in western North America during the Late Cretaceous. One of the largest known land predators, measuring up to 12.3 metres in length and weighing up to 8.4 tonnes. Tyrannosaurus possessed a massive skull with powerful jaw muscles capable of generating enormous bite force.

features: Massive skull with robust jaw musculature
Tiny, two-fingered forelimbs

diagnostic_features: Premaxillary teeth D-shaped in cross-section
Fused nasals
Reduced forelimb with two functional digits

species.name: Tyrannosaurus rex
species.description: The only widely accepted species of Tyrannosaurus. Among the last non-avian dinosaurs to exist before the Cretaceous-Paleogene extinction event.

references.title: Tyrannosaurus and other Cretaceous carnivorous dinosaurs
references.journal: Bulletin of the American Museum of Natural History

references.authors: Brusatte, S.L.; Norell, M.A.; Carr, T.D.; Erickson, G.M.; Hutchinson, J.R.; Balanoff, A.M.; Bever, G.S.; Choiniere, J.N.; Makovicky, P.J.; Xu, X.
references.title: Tyrannosaur paleobiology: new research on ancient exemplar organisms

Velociraptor.description: A genus of small dromaeosaurid dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous of Mongolia. Despite its popular depiction as a large predator, Velociraptor was roughly the size of a turkey, about 2 metres long and 15 kilograms. It possessed a large, sickle-shaped claw on the second toe of each foot, characteristic of dromaeosaurids.

features: Large, sickle-shaped retractable claw on second toe
Long, stiffened tail for balance
Elongated snout with serrated teeth

diagnostic_features: Depressed and elongated skull with upturned snout
Enlarged sickle claw on pedal digit II
Long, low skull with over 26 teeth in each maxilla

species.name: Velociraptor mongoliensis
species.description: The type species of Velociraptor. Known from numerous specimens discovered in the Gobi Desert, including the famous "Fighting Dinosaurs" specimen locked in combat with a Protoceratops.

references.title: Three new Theropoda, Protoceratops zone, central Mongolia

references.authors: Turner, A.H.; Makovicky, P.J.; Norell, M.A.
references.title: A review of dromaeosaurid systematics and paravian phylogeny
references.journal: Bulletin of the American Museum of Natural History
